entry.title: Customer & Partner Roundtable for SAP BTP ABAP Environment #25
entry.content:
👉 The call for contributions for one of the upcoming roundtables is open! 

 

If you want to show a demo or share a use case scenario for SAP BTP ABAP Environment send us an email and we will get back to you.
 

Introduction

 

As SAP BTP ABAP environment (aka Steampunk) and ABAP Cloud became more popular inside and outside of SAP, there is a high demand for rolling out the latest product news and updates, asking questions, and showing demos. 

💡 You can find the slides presented, recordings, and further references from the previous roundtables in this GitHub repository.

Meeting Information

When:                                                                      
  • July 31, 10:00 - 11:00 AM CET  – Zoom Meeting (please register in advance) 
 
Who:
  • All interested customers, partners, and stakeholders are invited to join this roundtable
  • BTP ABAP team:
    • Frank Jentsch (Product Lead for SAP BTP ABAP Environment)
    • Iwona Hahn (BTP ABAP Product Management)

  • Partner blue-zone GmbH: Andreas Klose (Director Software Development), Christof Unterste (Solution Architect) 

Preliminary Agenda:
  • Product update for SAP BTP ABAP Environment
  • Partner live demo: EDI-ZONE (EDI & eInvoicing Solution) - managed SaaS solution on SAP BTP ABAP Environment:  subaccount handling, customer onboarding etc.
  • Q&A 
Looking forward to meeting you!
 

 



Check out our SAP Business Technology ABAP Environment page in SAP Community for product updates and upcoming events.

entry.title: Re: RDP access point connection doesn't work in CAL Appli...
entry.content:
Hi @tsvetinka_gaydazhieva,

Thanks for your tip. I guess if I access the SAP CAL appliance in different PCs (different IPs) the restriction on IP Range configuration  lost itself. 

I did the following procedures and it works fine to solve this troble:

1. Into your SAP CAL Appliance, change to <Edit> mode and go to Access Points on Virtual Machines section. 

Clear Restrictions

The IP Range becomes empty

2. Click on <Restrict Access> to update the IP Range definitions.

On the pop-up screen, mark the option <My IP Adress> and click on <Restrict> button.

PS: The option "Custom IP Ranges" might works if you list the IP addresses that you used in your PCs.

The IP adress is updated on IP Range definition.

3. Save changes.

I am very grateful for any feedback!

I have setup everything by following all 3 parts of the series and I have also created a program that performs the steps

1)  Authenticate (get oauth auth. token)

2) Request the DOX client with the /clients  endpoint (I could have hardcoded "default")

entry.title: Simplifying Complex Enterprise Landscapes Using SAP Integration Suite – A Practical Approach
entry.content:
“Complexity is inevitable. Chaos isn’t.”

In today’s enterprise IT landscape, complexity is everywhere — hybrid architectures, ever-evolving business needs, legacy platforms, and disconnected systems. But complexity doesn’t have to mean confusion or fragility.

As a Senior SAP Integration Architect with over 11 years of experience across SAP CPI, PI/PO, BTP, and Ariba CIG, I’ve seen firsthand how integration complexity can slow innovation — and how the right architectural mindset can fix it.

This post is a practical guide to simplifying complex enterprise landscapes using the SAP Integration Suite.

🔹 Why Complexity Happens

Most organizations face at least one (if not all) of these challenges:

  • A mix of cloud and on-premise systems

  • Legacy, point-to-point integrations built without standards

  • Business logic scattered across different platforms

  • Siloed teams and lack of visibility across integrations

The result? High maintenance, fragile interfaces, slower time-to-market.

🔹 My Guiding Principles for Simplification

1️⃣ API-First Architecture

Design integrations as modular, discoverable services — not tightly coupled one-offs. Use SAP API Management to expose, secure, and govern your interfaces centrally.

2️⃣ Decouple Business Logic from Integration

Avoid embedding decision logic in CPI flows or PI/PO mappings. Move that logic to CAP apps, BRF+, or reusable business services — this reduces rework and improves clarity.

3️⃣ Think Event-Driven

Don’t force synchronous flows where asynchronous is better. Use SAP Event Mesh to build decoupled, reactive systems that scale more naturally.

4️⃣ Reuse Over Rebuild

Standardize error handling, logging, naming conventions, and message formats. Use templates, shared artifacts, and integration accelerators wherever possible.

5️⃣ Integration as a Platform

Treat integration as a long-term platform, not a project. Build with future growth, maintainability, and observability in mind.

🔹 Using SAP Integration Suite Holistically

Most teams focus only on SAP CPI — but the SAP Integration Suite is more powerful when used as a full toolkit:

  • Cloud Integration (CPI): For process orchestration across SAP and non-SAP systems

  • API Management: To secure, manage, and reuse interfaces

  • SAP Managed Gateway (Ariba CIG): For rapid, standardized B2B procurement integration

  • Event Mesh: To enable scalable, event-driven communication

  • SAP BTP Cockpit & Cloud ALM: For centralized monitoring, alerting, and operational control

Together, these tools can reduce integration effort, increase visibility, and support long-term agility.

🔹 Real-World Tactics That Worked for Me

Here are some of the steps I’ve implemented in customer landscapes:

  • Migrated legacy PI/PO integrations to CPI on SAP BTP

  • Created custom adapters and reusable Groovy-based modules

  • Designed a central error-handling and alerting framework

  • Introduced integration naming standards, templates, and design guidelines

  • Used Cloud ALM to monitor interfaces across landscapes

  • Built a knowledge base of reusable integration components

🔹 Final Thoughts

Complexity is natural. But without intentional design, it turns into chaos.

By adopting a product mindset, focusing on API-first, reusable, and event-driven architecture, and fully utilizing the SAP Integration Suite, we can turn fragile systems into future-ready platforms.

Let’s not just connect systems — let’s design integration that scales with the product, not against it.

🔁 Let’s Discuss

How are you approaching simplification in your integration landscape?
Would love to hear your views and lessons from the field.

entry.title: Re: Use of Settlement Material in Settlement Management
entry.content:
Hi @Dforway1 

The "No Material Number Passed On" error typically occurs in scenarios where the system cannot find any business volume during the execution of the settlement. In such cases, the system may expect a Settlement Material to proceed with creating the settlement document.

That said, a Settlement Material is not required by default, unless it has been made mandatory via configuration. You can also configure a default Settlement Material at the Condition Contract Type level, using local configuration, to avoid manual entry on every contract.

Regards, 
Gauthier Victor

entry.title: Re: Why SAP Cloud Identity Matters for SAP Task Center: It’s About Trust and Global User ID
entry.content:
Hi Steve,

Good point. There is no difference between Identity Authentication version 2 and Identity Directory when they are used as source, target, or proxy systems. Both expose the same API: The Identity Directory API (SAP Business Accelerator Hub). The key difference is the following:

  • Identity Directory: When you provision entities to Identity Directory, they are written to the identity directory of your current SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant. Therefore, no connection and authentication properties (such as URL, Password, User, Authentication, etc.) need to be set.

  • Identity Authentication Version 2: When provisioning entities to Identity Authentication version 2, you need to configure the connection properties to point to the desired SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant.

Hope this clarifies the use case.

On the other hand, Identity Authentication version 1 is different and exposes a different API: Identity Authentication: SCIM REST API

Best regards,

Ivelina

entry.title: SAP Build Process Automation – Configuring Microsoft 365 email provider for SMTP mail destination
entry.content:
A common use case for SAP Build Process Automation is email communication to end users. To implement the Email step in your processes, you need both an active mail server and a properly configured destination in your SAP BTP subaccount.

Currently, SAP Build Process Automation supports three authentication methods for email services:

  • Basic Authentication
  • OAuth2 Password Grant
  • OAuth2 Client Credentials Flow

It's important to note that Microsoft 365 no longer supports Basic Authentication, having shifted to more secure authentication protocols. While Basic Authentication remains viable with other email providers, Microsoft environments require alternative approaches. For those interested, the OAuth2 Password Flow implementation has been documented in a previous article.

This guide will walk you through the comprehensive steps needed to configure a Microsoft 365 account as an SMTP mail destination using the OAuth2 Client Credentials Flow, following the official documentation on configuring SMTP Mail Destinations. One benefit of using this grant flow, is that Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) can remain enabled.

Prerequisites

  • A tenant in Microsoft Azure Entra ID (Active Directory).
  • A user with an active Outlook 365 subscription.

 

Microsoft Azure configuration

Along those steps, I will be using a new tenant, with unconfigured users and will manage that using the admin account.

Create a Microsoft Azure Active Directory Application

Step 1: Access the Azure Portal.

Step 2: Open Microsoft Entra Id.

Step 3: Navigate to Manage and then select App Registrations.

Step 4: Select New Registration.

Step 5: Provide an application name, select Accounts in any organizational directory (Any Microsoft Entra ID tenant - Multitenant) and register.

On the following page, you will find the TenantId and ClientId. Save them, they will be needed.

Step 6: We will now create the SecretId. Under Manage, select Certificates & Secrets.

Step 7: Create a New client secret. Add a description and select an expiration date.

Save the secret value generated, this will be used as your SecretId.

Step 8: Let's now add the correct API permission. Go under Manage and select API permissions.

Step 9: Select Add a permission.

Step 10: Select APIs my organization uses.

Step 11: Search for Office 365 Exchange Online and select it.

Step 12: Select Application permissions and search for SMTP.SendAsApp, select it and add the permission.

Step 13: Now Grant admin consent for Contoso.

You should see the status updated as followed:

We are now done for the configuration of the application. We will now configure the user.

 

Configure Admin Center to enable SMTP Auth

As mentioned at the beginning, the SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is the standard protocol used for sending email. We will now configure the user to authorize it.

Step 14: Access Microsoft admin portal. 

Step 15: Expand Users and select Active Users.

Step 16: Select the user you want to configure.

Step 17: Under Mail, select Manage email apps.

Step 18: Make sure the box Authenticated SMTP is checked, if not, check it and save.

The configuration of the user is now done, there is now one more step before you can send mails.

 

Register service principals in Exchange

We will now be using the powershell to execute the commands from this learn Microsoft page: Authenticate an IMAP, POP or SMTP connection using OAuth.

Step 19: Make sure you're running your powershell as administrator. Then, install and import the ExchangeOnlineManagement module.

Install-Module -Name ExchangeOnlineManagement
Import-module ExchangeOnlineManagement

Step 20: Connect to ExchangeOnline using your TenantId, the one saved from earlier (step 5).

Connect-ExchangeOnline -Organization <tenantId>

A pop-up window should open asking you for your credentials and your authentication code. Once signed-in, you should see the above.

Step 21: Register the Microsoft Entra application's service principal in Exchange.

New-ServicePrincipal -AppId <APPLICATION_ID> -ObjectId <OBJECT_ID>

To find the ApplicationId and ObjectId, you will have to go back to the Azure Portal, go to Microsoft Entra ID and Enterprise Applications under Manage.

Select the application you've created earlier and use the following ids.

Once runned, you should see:

Step 22: Add now the specific mailboxes in the tenant that will be allowed to be accessed by your application. Use the user from step 16. This command might take time to reflect and can take up to several hours to be propagated into Microsoft's system, so don't rush into the testing 😉. It took ~1 hour in this guide for this step to be applied.

Add-MailboxPermission -Identity "<YOUR_USER>" -User <OBJECT_ID> -AccessRights FullAccess

Done, you should be all set ! Let's now test it (considering you've waited long enough).

 

SAP Build Process Automation configuration and testing

Add all the information you've got from the steps above in your destination, then save.

To get the properties correct, follow the SAP portal. Also note that for the OAuth2 Client Credentials flow, the scope is: https://outlook.office.com/.default.

You can now go under Control Tower, select Mail Server, click on Send Test Mail.

Choose your recipient, press Send Test Mail.

 

And voilà:

entry.title: Re: Current Preparation Plan for C_S4CFI
entry.content:
Hi Benjamin,
the exam potentially changes with each release of SAP S/4HANA Cloud. This is the history as far as I am able to reconstruct it:
C_S4CFI_2504 - exam includes beginner learning journeys related to Finance
C_S4CFI_2408 - exam includes foundational content and a mix of beginner and intermediate learning journeys related to Finance. This was a strategic decision that was taken for all certifications related to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. 
C_S4CFI_2504 - exam includes foundational content (as before) and intermediate learning journeys related to Finance. With this, we reached what we've been striving for for a while. The certification is planned to remain in this shape. Of course you always have to expect small adjustments inside the courses or that new courses might be added or exchanged. But basically, this certification should be quite stable now. 

I hope this helps. Best regards, Doris.

entry.title: Announcement: SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud Availability via SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) is a fully managed SaaS solution that unifies and governs all SAP data and seamlessly connects with third-party data, giving line-of-business leaders context to make even more impactful decisions.

Learn more: SAP Business Data Cloud | SAP

SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud are core component services of BDC.  Both services remain strategic to SAP and will continue to be enhanced through their own roadmaps, offered via SAP Business Data Cloud.

SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud are scheduled to be removed from the list of Eligible Cloud Services for new BTPEA, CPEA, and PAYG subscriptions as of January 1, 2026.  

What does this mean for customers? 

These services will be available until the end of a customer’s current BTPEA, CPEA, or PAYG agreement terms.  After December 31, 2025, these services will not be available for renewal. While the services will not be available for renewal terms, the same services will continue to be available in SAP Business Data Cloud.

Existing BTPEA and CPEA customers who purchase cloud credits before January 1, 2026, will be able to continue to use their cloud credits for SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud for the duration of their agreements.

Although customers can continue their current BTPEA, CPEA, and PAYG agreements, there is no need to wait to move to SAP Business Data Cloud.  Customers can now begin their transition plans to SAP BDC.  Please contact your SAP Account team to support your plans.

Note: Existing SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud tenants will be preserved under SAP BDC, no technical migration required. 

 

SAP Analytics Cloud in this blog refers to the following:

  • SAP Analytics Cloud for business intelligence, public system option
  • SAP Analytics Cloud for planning, standard edition, public system option
  • SAP Analytics Cloud for planning, professional edition, public system option
  • SAP Analytics Cloud, test tenant, public system option

entry.title: Re: Restrict Manage Banks, Manage Banks Cash Management,...
entry.content:
Thank you for reaching out. To implement a display only restriction for the following apps:  

- Manage Bank Account  
- Manage Bank - Cash Management  
- Manage Bank - Master Data  

You will need to Create a New business role and assign the relevant business catalogs to it. The catalogs to include are:  

- SAP_FIN_BC_CM_BAM2_BASIC_PC (Manage Bank Account)  
- SAP_FIN_BC_CM_BNK_PC (Manage Bank - Cash Management)  
- SAP_CA_BC_BNK_PC (Manage Bank - Master Data)  

When a new business role is created, the default access category is set to No Access. This means the role will have display-only permissions without any write authorizations. Additionally, the default status for the Read access category is Unrestricted.  

To enable these restrictions, assign your user to the newly created business role. Once assigned, they will have default read-only access to the specified apps.  

If you have any further questions or require additional clarification, I’m here to assist.

Thank you for your cooperation.

entry.title: Re: SAC | Passing URL Parameters from Optimized Story to Data Analyzer
entry.content:
@bw_micha there a different ways on how you can fill the Connection Parameter, depending on what is needed.

If using SAC Model: 
// If using SAC Models you can use 'undefined'
NavigationUtils.openDataAnalyzer(undefined, "My Model Name", undefined, true);
If using a Connection (for example BW4):
// If using a Live-Connection like for example BW4
NavigationUtils.openDataAnalyzer("Connection Name", "Technical Query Name", undefined, true);

Connection Name: Here you have different ways to get the Name, depending on which Authorization you have. (depending on that i suggest you use point 2 or 3)

  1. Using the Main Navigation Menu in SAP Analytics Cloud -> Connections. Look for your connection in the List like for example BW4 (depending on how you named it in the system). Here the Name field should show the connection identifier you need.
  2.  Go to Files -> Create ("+" Icon) and create a Model. Here select Live Data Model and then for example System Type "SAP BW".  Then select connection and choose the System you want to use. In the list (left side and not Description) as well as after selecting it, it shows you the Name you need to use. (Example "BW4")
  3. Go to a existing Model in your Files which uses the same Connection you need. Open it and click on the "Wrench"-Icon -> Preferences. In the General Model Settings you now have the Description, System and BEx Query. For the Connection Name use the System displayed there.

Additional Notes: 

  1. The Connection Name is case-sensitive so please look out.
  2. In the Connections List it is the display name and not a technical ID.

For the Model Name in this case it is the technical id of the BEx Query. For example shown in Step 3 under the point BEx Query.

If using Datasphere: Example for a Analytical Model (Cube)
// If using Datasphere as a Connection
NavigationUtils.openDataAnalyzer(
    "Datasphere Connection Name",  // Your Datasphere connection name
    "[XXX][][YYYYYYYY]",  //[SPACE][][Name of Analytical Model (Cube)]
    UrlParameter.create("systemType", "DATASPHERE"), //Needed Parameter
    true  // Open in new tab
);

For the Datasphere Connection Name it also depends on how it was named in your System. Easy way for example to get it is using the SAP Analytics Cloud Main Menu -> Data Analyzer -> From Existing Model. On the left side when selecting a Model you can directly see the Datasphere Systems which are connected to your System. Just use the Name showing there.

The "[XXX][][YYYYYYYY]" is then depending on your System, while keeping the Brackets. (Only replace the XXX or YYYYYYYY). XXX stands for the desired Space in the DSP System, while YYYYYYYY stands for example for the Analytical Model (Cube). Length of YYYYYYYY is depending on your Naming Convention and doesnt have to be 8 Symbols like shown here.

"At the [Branch Name] branch, there is a special discount."

Can I create this using a personalization rule that pulls the branch name and discount dynamically for each customer?
Or do I need to create a separate SMS and newsletter for each branch and manually assign the relevant customers to each version using a segment?

Thanks in advance,


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Personalization Rule Based on Sales Table for SMS and...
entry.content:
As of now, currently it is not possible to directly fetch fields from the sales data table for personalization purposes. In order to achieve this scenario, you have a few different approaches:

  • Create a custom field to store the branch details: with that, you can use simple contact data personalization tokens, but it would require creating a new field and updating all your contacts to have their preferred branch assigned;

  • Use relational data token: if you use relational data and have the preferred branch of each contact stored, you can create a personalization token based on the relational data value for the branch:
    • SAP Help documentation references: Running personalization queries on your database | SAP Help Portal, Personalizing Your Relational Data | SAP Help Portal and Creating Relational Data tokens | SAP Help Portal 

  • Use external data token: similar to the relational data token, with external data tokens you can fetch the branch data from your other systems using an API call:
    • SAP Help documentation references: External Content - Overview | SAP Help Portal and Creating External Content tokens | SAP Help Portal 

 

I also suggest you to submit this improvement as a feature request through SAP Influence so that the feature could be evaluated by product management and possibly implemented in future releases. Please refer to KBA 3367696 - Enhancement Request Process for SAP Emarsys Using the Customer Influence Platform.

 

Best regards,

SAP Technical Support

entry.title: SII tip for Spain #1: <NumSerieFacturaEmisor> in outgoing invoices
entry.content:
Welcome to the SII tips for Spain. In this and subsequent short blogs, we will provide different tips on special SII features in relation to the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance solution.

The tip for today is a recurring topic we are receiving from many customers.

One of the key fields when submitting outgoing invoices to the SII in the Spanish Tax Agency is the <NumSerieFacturaEmisor> (invoice number). According to the Invoicing regulations (art. 6) ,  the invoice number must be "correlative"., which means: sequential (1,2,3,4…), chronological (date of doc 1 cannot be later than date of doc 2) and without gaps.

In case of issued invoices, the field <NumSerieFacturaEmisor> is calculated by the system based on the ODN process (Official Document Number) and picked from the Reference field (XBLNR), provided that the XBLNR field is customized accordingly in SSCUI 102557, or view FIESSII_DOCTYPEV. The ODN numbers will be generated in Sales, and only via billing process (VF01) for Spain. The accounting documents generated for each billing document will be posted with the ODN number in the Reference field (XBLNR).

It is important to mention, that if you create an accounting document via FB70 directly in FI, the ODN functionality will not be triggered and therefore the reference field (XBLNR) will either be empty or with the information you manually put in it.  The reason is that "invoices" as such are not conceptually generated in Accounting (FI), but in Sales (SD).  Enabling ODN in FI and in SD will make the invoicing numbering not compliant with the Invoicing regulations in Spain.

Sometimes, the invoices are generated externally and transferred to SAP through the accounting interface. In these cases, you need to ensure that the Reference field (XBLNR) in the accounting document is externally populated correctly, so that the SII field <NumSerieFacturaEmisor> has the right value.

In case of additional questions, you can visit the SII help documentation in the following links:

  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Private Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in ERP

Other tips:

  • SII tip for Spain #2: Reuse <NumSerieFacturaEmisor> in case of de-registered invoices

entry.title: Come join us for the SAP TechEd Community Cluetrain 2025 edition
entry.content:
For me, personally, SAP TechEd 2008 in Berlin, is where the Community really began for me. 
While I also attended the 2007 TechEd, TechEd 2008 in Berlin is the conference that will always be on the top of my list over unforgettable conferences attended. 

TechEd Berlin 2008 is where I met many people I still consider friends today for the first time face-to-face, the Community Clubhouse was the go-to-place, and I got to experience being on the DemoJam stage with Dick Hirsch demoing the best community-led project ever (shameless self-promotion); ESME:

If you were there in 2008, you will perhaps also recognise many faces in this video from Dennis Howlett: 


Another highlight of TechEd 2008 for me, was the SAP TechEd Cluetrain that @craigcmehil arranged. 
Since the 2025 TechEd in Berlin is all about the SAP Community coming home for me, I thought why not also relaunch the Cluetrain this year?

In 2008 we were about 25 Community members and friends who got together, and took the train together from Frankfurt to Berlin to get the conversations started. 
@gregorw published this post, and @JimSpath shared this post, about the trip. 

This year we will meet at Frankfurt Main Hbf (Frankfurt Main Central station), and take the train departing Monday, November 3rd at 13:14 CET (1:14 pm) together: 

This time we will need to try to self-coordinate buying tickets individually so that we end up sitting in the same coach. 
To start I have already booked my ticket - you will find me in coach 4, seat 91. 
When you book your tickets, please make sure you book the ticket with seat reservation and pick a seat in coach 4:

I will be at the central station about an hour before the train departs. I will add a comment on this blog post as we get closer which café you can find me in. 

After arriving in Berlin, the idea is that we will all move on to attend a SAP Stammtisch together. 
Our Berlin group owner, @rofinke, is out on vacation right now, so time and place is still to be announced. 
@rofinke - FYI for when you are back, we will host a Stammtisch together on November 3rd 😁  (please check your private messages when you are back online)

Big shout out to @gregorw - as always, thank you for your support!

I will end this post with the same shoutout that Craig did in 2008:
Several hundred kilometers of conversation, community, collaboration and co-innovation! 
I hope to see many of you on the train 🙂
(if you book a ticket, let us know by adding a comment below)

  • SAP TechEd registration is now live, you can read more about it here.
  • DemoJam is back this year as well, check out this post from @cecihuergo 
  • Aaaand the Community Talks are back as well

#sapcommunityiscominghome 🥳

entry.title: A Comprehensive Guide to Transition from SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition to advanced edition
entry.content:
As organizations evolve, the need for more integrated digital workplace solutions becomes paramount. SAP Build Work Zone offers two distinct editions to cater to varying organizational needs: the standard edition and the advanced edition. While the standard edition provides a centralized access point to multiple SAP systems, the advanced edition extends these capabilities by empowering business users. This guide aims to facilitate a seamless transition from the standard to the advanced edition, ensuring that your organization can fully harness the enhanced features of the advanced edition.

Understanding the Differences

Before embarking on the transition, it's essential to comprehend the key distinctions between the two editions to make the most of the expanded set of capabilities in advanced:

  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition: Formerly known as SAP Launchpad service, this edition provides a central access point to multiple SAP cloud and on-premise applications, processes & tasks via different ‘application site’. It also offers different customization and extension capabilities. 

SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition extends the capabilities of the standard edition by empowering business users to create, manage, and publish their own content. It also offers a broader set of widgets for more flexible workpage building. Users can also create interactive communities, known as workspaces, with document access, discussion forums, knowledge base, task collaboration & more.

The following visual offers a more detailed comparison, expanded feature set of the SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition. 

Subscription and Service Plan differences

  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition is often bundled with certain SAP cloud solutions (such as SAP S/4HANA Cloud), providing a streamlined setup for foundational portal and page-building use cases. It supports both subscription-based and consumption-based licensing models, making it suitable for customers seeking fast onboarding with minimal complexity. You can find more details here.
  • On the other hand, SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition is a standalone, premium offering that unlocks enterprise-grade capabilities. It requires a separate license and subscription that follows a follows a consumption-based commercial model. Read more here.

Transition Steps

Transitioning from the standard to the advanced edition involves several critical steps: 

1. Assess current implementation

Begin by thoroughly evaluating your existing SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition setup. Document all active sites, pages, applications, and integrations. Identify any custom developments, branding elements, or specific configurations that are crucial for replication or adaptation in the advanced edition.

2. Plan the transition

Establish clear objectives for the transition, such as enhancing user engagement or improving content management. Engage key stakeholders, including IT teams, business users, and content creators, to gather requirements and address potential concerns.

3. Exporting content from standard edition

It's crucial to export your existing content from standard edition to facilitate a smooth transition to the advanced edition. In the Site Directory, select the desired site, click on 'More Options', and choose 'Export Site' to download the site and its content as a ZIP file. This export includes the site's structure, pages, and associated applications, ensuring that essential components are preserved for import into the advanced edition.

4. Set up the advanced edition environment

It is possible to subscribe to both SAP Build Work Zone, standard and advanced editions within the same subaccount. This makes it easier to set up the advanced edition without disrupting your current standard edition setup.

To get started, subscribe to SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition through the SAP BTP Cockpit. Configure user authentication and provisioning using SAP Cloud Identity Services (Identity Authentication and Identity Provisioning) to ensure secure and efficient user management.

Use the SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition booster to automate many of the initial setup steps in your subaccount. In addition to the booster, advanced edition requires a set of 'configurator' steps to be completed. These are essential for enabling full functionality of the service (and are not required or present in the standard edition). These steps typically include setting up roles, permissions, and other advanced configurations tailored to the edition’s extended capabilities.

5. Content migration

Since there is no fully automated upgrade or migration flow from the standard to the advanced edition, manual intervention is required. To ease the transition, you can export most of the content from the standard edition and import it into the advanced edition. This process involves the following steps:

  • Exporting the necessary content from the standard edition (as described above)
  • Importing the content to advanced edition

This approach allows for optimization and restructuring where needed. Additionally, leveraging the advanced edition's capabilities, such as interactive workspaces and enhanced page-building tools, can enrich the user experience.

6. Reusing existing configurations

When subscribing to the advanced edition within the same subaccount, certain configurations can be seamlessly reused. BTP destinations, Cloud Connector configurations, and custom applications remain intact and do not require (full) reconfiguration.

If your organization utilizes a custom domain, this can significantly ease the transition from a user perspective. For example, a custom domain that previously pointed to your standard edition site can now be reconfigured to point to the new advanced edition site. This allows users to continue accessing the Work Zone through their existing bookmarks and saved links without disruption. It ensures continuity and minimizes confusion during the switch, while maintaining consistent access points throughout the transition.

7. Best practices

To ensure a smooth transition it's advisable to first perform all migration and configuration activities in a non-production environment, such as a development or testing subaccount. This approach allows for thorough testing, validation of configurations, and identification of potential issues without impacting the live production environment. Once the transition process has been successfully tested and refined in these lower environments, you can confidently replicate the steps in the production subaccount, minimizing the risk of disruptions to end-users.

8. Testing and validation

Conduct thorough User Acceptance Testing (UAT) with a group of end-users to ensure all functionalities work as intended and meet business requirements. Evaluate the system's performance under typical load conditions to identify and address any potential issues.

9. Change management

Develop and deliver training sessions to familiarize users with the new features and interfaces of the advanced edition. Provide documentation, FAQs, and support channels to assist users during the transition period.

10. Go-live

Execute the transition plan, ensuring minimal disruption to daily operations. Continuously monitor the system for any issues and gather user feedback to make necessary adjustments.

Conclusion

This transition can significantly enhance your organization's digital workplace capabilities. By following a structured approach and leveraging the advanced features, you can create a more engaging and efficient user experience. It can empower your teams with the tools they need to collaborate effectively and drive business success.

Hope this blogpost will support your transition journey. We’ll be following up shortly with a detailed how-to guide to help you through the process. 

For more detailed information consider exploring the following:

  • SAP Help Portal: Getting Started with SAP Build Work Zone
  • SAP Build Work Zone on: sap.com / SAP Community
  • SAP Learning Journeys:  Implement and Administer SAP Build Work Zone

entry.title: SII tip for Spain #2: Reuse <NumSerieFacturaEmisor> in case of de-registered invoices
entry.content:
Welcome to the SII tips for Spain. In this and subsequent short blogs, we will provide different tips on special SII features in relation to the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance solution.

The tip for today is a recurring topic we are receiving from many customers.

Sometimes it is necessary to cancel one invoice and create a new one. The question is whether it is possible to reuse the same invoice number in the field <NumSerieFacturaEmisor> .

For example, if we create the document number 241000000000112025 with invoice number "0001" ; then reverse the document; finally, create a new document 241000000000132025 with invoice number "0001", the Tax Agency SII will return an error message 3000 "Duplicated invoice" when submitting document 241000000000132025.

The reason is that, according to the Tax Agency documentation, the invoice de-registration is a logical process (by mark) and not a physical deletion (so the invoice still exists in the database), and therefore, the combination <NIF><NumSerieFacturaEmisor><FechaExpedicionFacturaEmisor> is still in use.

If you then want to register an invoice again with the same <NumSerieFacturaEmisor> , you need to change either the Issuer's Tax ID Number (<NIF>), or the issue date (<FechaExpedicionFacturaEmisor>).

Check the following Tax Agency link for additional information:  If an invoice is cancelled, can another one be registered with the same invoice issuer series number?

The system will not perform any check on the invoice number of the new document (typically stored in the accounting document Reference field, XBLNR).

 

In case of additional questions, you can visit the SII help documentation in the following links:

  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Private Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in ERP

Previous SII tip:

  • SII tip for Spain #1: <NumSerieFacturaEmisor> in outgoing invoices

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud ALM Integration with Power BI or other thir...
entry.content:
Hello,

it is technically possible to consume APIs from CALM in PowerBI, but it's a bit "messy". 

First of all, you have to perform OAuth authentication "manually" in the query code. As well, if you use REST feed with lots of pages you might need to handle the pagination as well in the code (ODATA handles pagination automatically for you).

Disclaimer: what I'm going to share was done using AI tools and web search, so it is possibly re-using code from others. I'm not claiming any ownership of this. As well, I'm definitely not a BI or PowerBI expert, so I did just things o I could get the data I wanted in the tool.

The example was getting Tasks info from CALM. There are 2 APIs for that, one REST (Overview | SAP Cloud ALM Tasks | SAP Business Accelerator Hub) and one OData one for Analytics (SAP Business Accelerator Hub). Unfortunately, none of them had all the info we needed.

To proceed, you'll need an instance of "SAP Cloud ALM API" with the proper scopes assigned (I'm not going to discuss that, the CALM API doc has all the details on scopes needed for each API), and a Service Key from this instance.

In PowerBI, I created a "function" querry  called GetToken() with this code to do the Oauth Authentication:

() => let
        ClientID="<ClientID from SK>",
        ClientSecret="<ClientSecret from SK>",
        TokenResponse = Json.Document(Web.Contents("<Token URL from SK>", [Headers = [#"Content-Type" = "application/x-www-form-urlencoded"],Content = Text.ToBinary("grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=" & ClientID & "&client_secret=" & ClientSecret)])),
        AccessToken = TokenResponse[access_token]
    in
        AccessToken

Then to consume the OData API, I created a "Blank Query" and added this code:

let
    SelectStatement="firstWeekDay,period,resolution,timeZone,timestamp,timestampFormat,date,week,dayOfWeek,project,projectName,scope,scopeName,parentTask,name,status,statusText,phase,role,type,typeID,priority,processor,taskGUID,taskID,overdue,dueDate,timeboxName,team,release,plannedCompDate,actualCompDate,counter,storyPoint,effort",
  ProjectID="<your project ID>",
  Token=GetToken(),
  Source = OData.Feed("https://<your CALM tenant URL>/api/calm-analytics/v1/odata/v4/analytics/Tasks?$filter=period eq 'C1D' and resolution eq 'D' and project eq '" & ProjectID & "'&$select=" & SelectStatement, [Authorization = "Bearer " & Token], [Implementation = "2.0", ODataVersion = 4])
in
    Source

You would have to edit this code for the relevant SelectStatement and  other filters, etc... in the URL

For the REST API, here you need to implement pagination, with another "Blank Query" and code like this:

let
    Token=GetToken(),
    // Define the base URL
    BaseUrl = "<URL of your CALM tenant>/api/calm-tasks/v1/tasks?projectId=<your project GUID>&limit=1000&offset=",

    // Function to get data from a specific page
    GetPage = (PageNumber as number) =>
        let
            Source = Json.Document(Web.Contents(BaseUrl & Text.From(PageNumber),[Headers = [Authorization = "Bearer " & Token]]))
            //Data = Source[data]
        in
            Source,

    // Function to get data from all pages
    GetAllPages = (PageNumber as number, AccumulatedData as list) =>
        let
            CurrentPageData = GetPage(PageNumber),
            NewAccumulatedData = List.Combine({AccumulatedData, CurrentPageData}),
            NextPageNumber = PageNumber + 1000,
            MoreData = if List.Count(CurrentPageData) > 0 then @GetAllPages(NextPageNumber, NewAccumulatedData) else NewAccumulatedData
        in
            MoreData,

    // Start the pagination from page 1
    source = GetAllPages(1, {}),
    #"Converted to Table" = Table.FromList(source, Splitter.SplitByNothing(), null, null, ExtraValues.Error),
    #"Expanded Column1" = Table.ExpandRecordColumn(#"Converted to Table", "Column1", {"id", "projectId", "title", "type", "status", "source", "externalId", "startDate", "dueDate", "priorityId", "assigneeId", "assigneeName", "parentId", "subStatus", "approvalState", "scopeId", "scopeName", "assignedRoleId", "assignedRoleName", "storyPoints", "obsolete", "workstream", "deliverableId", "responsibleTeamId", "createdTimestamp", "lastChangedTimestamp", "effort", "involvedParties", "displayId", "tags", "customField1", "customField2", "customField3", "customField4", "customField5", "customField6", "customField7", "customField8", "customField9", "customField10", "customField11", "customField12", "customField13", "customField14", "customField15"}, {"id", "projectId", "title", "type", "status", "source", "externalId", "startDate", "dueDate", "priorityId", "assigneeId", "assigneeName", "parentId", "subStatus", "approvalState", "scopeId", "scopeName", "assignedRoleId", "assignedRoleName", "storyPoints", "obsolete", "workstream", "deliverableId", "responsibleTeamId", "createdTimestamp", "lastChangedTimestamp", "effort", "involvedParties", "displayId", "tags", "customField1", "customField2", "customField3", "customField4", "customField5", "customField6", "customField7", "customField8", "customField9", "customField10", "customField11", "customField12", "customField13", "customField14", "customField15"})
in
    #"Expanded Column1"

Again, you might want to adapt based on which fields you want to use or not, etc... It goes by pages of 1000 records. If you want to change page size you might have to do it in several places (code is not optimized).

This is not a very nice solution, especially with the credentials in the code of GetToken(), and maybe there are better ways to do this... but at least it works.

Note that you can use the same "code" in Excel instead of PowerBI.

entry.title: SII tip for Spain #3: Error 2006. Invoice or Operation breakdown?
entry.content:
Welcome to the SII tips for Spain. In this and subsequent short blogs, we will provide different tips on special SII features in relation to the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance solution.

The tip for today is a very specific topic which is easy to solve.

Sometimes the tax agency rejects an invoice with the error 2006: The invoice contains an invoice breakdown whereas it should have an operation breakdown, as the invoice is not simplified or a summary entry and the Contraparte contains an IdOtro or a NIF that starts with an N (La factura contiene un desglose a nivel de factura cuando le corresponde un desglose a nivel de operación, por ser una factura no simplificada ni asiento resumen y la contraparte contiene un IdOtro o un Tax ID Number (NIF) que empiece por N).

When an ordinary invoice is reported to SII, the VAT information is reported under the XML tag  <DesgloseFactura>. However, in case of sales to non-residents (those with Spanish Tax ID Number, NIF, starting by “N”), the Tax Agency SII expects the VAT information to be reported under the XML tag <DesgloseTipoOperacion>.

In order to achieve this, you can use the IMG activity "Assign SII Attributes to Tax Codes" (view FIESSII_TAXCODEV) or SSCUI 102558.

Select the relevant tax code and mark “SaleNonRes” as “1” for sales of goods or “2” for sales of services.

 

 

In case of additional questions, you can visit the SII help documentation in the following links:

  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Private Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in ERP

Other SII tips: 

  • SII tip for Spain #2: Reuse <NumSerieFacturaEmisor> in case of de-registered invoices

entry.title: Re: Internal Reconciliation for BP with Both Customer and...
entry.content:
Hello Silambarasan,

Please follow below steps and you can knock off customer and vendor items against each other for same business partner.

Here's a breakdown of the process:

1. Master Data Setup:
Link Customer and Vendor Roles:
In the Business Partner (BP) master data, ensure that both customer and vendor roles are assigned to the same business partner. 

Activate Clearing Options: (This is a critical step)
In the Vendor Master: In the company code data, activate the "Clearing with customer" option. 

In the Customer Master: In the company code data, activate the "Clearing with vendor" option. 

These settings allow the system to recognize that the customer and vendor items can be cleared against each other. 

2. Clearing Transactions: You have 2 options - Automatic or Manual
A: Automatic Clearing (F.13 or Fiori app):
Use transaction F.13 or the corresponding Fiori app for automatic clearing. 

Specify the company code, customer, and vendor numbers, and define clearing criteria like assignment, reference, currency, or special G/L indicators. 

The system will then automatically clear the open items that match your criteria. 

B: Manual Clearing (F-44 for vendors, F-32 for customers):
For manual clearing, use transactions F-44 (for vendors) or F-32 (for customers). 

Enter the relevant vendor or customer account, company code, and currency. 

The system will display the open items. You can then select the items you want to clear and post the clearing document. 
I guess this might answer your question.

If you find this post useful, I kindly request you to award kudos as a small gesture. This motivates me to serve you better.

Appreciate your feedback.

Thanks,

Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Can we get the Payment medium flat file in table view
entry.content:
Hello Kavitha,

Here's a breakdown of how to download payment medium format data in  as an Excel file:-

1. Accessing Payment Medium Data:

Automatic Payment Transaction (F110):

Navigate to the Automatic Payment Transaction: Environment -> Payment medium -> Data Medium Administration.
Select your criteria and run the overview (F8).
Choose your desired payment file.

Manage Payment Media App (F1868):

Use this app to download payment media manually.
The download path is determined by your web browser settings.
 
2. Downloading and Exporting to Excel:

Downloading the File:

After selecting your payment file (using either method above), choose Edit -> Download or use Shift + F6.
Alternatively, right-click and select Download.
Specify the file name and choose a target folder on your local machine.
Validate the download.

Exporting to Spreadsheet Format:

Once the report is generated, select the System dropdown from the menu.
Choose List -> Save -> Save.
In the pop-up window, select the preferred file format, which is likely XLSX (Excel) in this case.
Generate the report, input a file name, and save it in the desired location.

Alternative Exporting Methods:

Direct Export from List View: Select the List menu, then Export -> Spreadsheet.
Using the Export Button: Click the export icon (arrow pointing right) and select the spreadsheet option.
Right-Click and Export: Right-click within the displayed data and select the spreadsheet option.

Important Notes:

The Data Medium Exchange (DME) file itself is typically a TXT file. You are essentially exporting the data displayed or generated by the payment medium program into an Excel spreadsheet format.
You might need to adjust the layout of the exported data in Excel to fit your needs.
Some reports or list views might have restrictions on exporting to certain formats or quantities.

If you find this post useful, I kindly request you to award kudos as a small gesture. This motivates me to serve you better.

Appreciate your feedback.

Thanks,

Divyesh

entry.title: Say Hi to Joule in SAP SuccessFactors Mobile, Powered by SAP Build - Part 1: Unlocking AI for HR
entry.content:
This two-part blog series explores how SAP SuccessFactors Mobile introduced SAP Joule - SAP's AI copilot - into the hands of mobile users. Part one introduces the opportunity, motivation, and foundational changes that made this integration possible. Part two explores the architectural evolution and how SAP Mobile Services enabled the delivery of this new experience.

SAP SuccessFactors is a market-leading Human Capital Management (HCM) solution, trusted by over 9,900 organizations and more than 260 million users globally. Designed to help organizations attract, develop, and retain their workforce, SAP SuccessFactors offers a comprehensive suite of tools to manage the entire employee lifecycle. 

Complementing the web experience, SAP SuccessFactors Mobile brings HR closer to employees - driving higher engagement, productivity, and informed decision-making. It delivers a native, consumer-grade experience while meeting strict enterprise-grade security standards. With performance and usability at its core, the app is tailored to meet the demands of modern mobile users. 

SAP Joule is an AI copilot grounded in customers' business data and infused with AI agents to proactively assist employees in every cloud application they use - while automating complex processes. 

With the 1H2025 release, SuccessFactors has introduced the Joule experience in its mobile app. This means employees can now interact with the entire enterprise suite using natural language directly from the SuccessFactors mobile app - whether they want to find information, complete a task, or navigate to the right location. 

Joule supports three primary capabilities for HR scenarios: transactional, navigational, and informational.  

  • In transactional scenarios, Joule not only retrieves data - it facilitates actions. For example, when a user says, "I want to request feedback from my manager," Joule collects the necessary context and sends the request directly within the same conversational thread. 
  • In navigational cases, Joule acts as a smart shortcut, directing users to the appropriate screen inside the app. For instance, if you’re unsure where to assign team goals, Joule will generate a direct deep link to the right page. 
  • For informational scenarios, Joule queries its underlying documentation and knowledge base, then presents a concise summary along with related resources. Ask it about your company’s leave policy, and it will display a brief overview plus the top three relevant documents. 

Joule in the SuccessFactors Mobile app isn't limited to HR scenarios. Users can also query data from connected systems like SAP S/4HANA or Ariba - for example, to view open sales orders or pending purchase requests. 

Joule can even be launched via Siri on iOS. Users can say, “Hey Siri, ask Joule in SuccessFactors…,” which opens the app and passes the query to Joule. Joule then processes and responds within the app - creating a seamless, hands-free experience. 

With SuccessFactors' capabilities rapidly expanding on the web, customers will now begin to see the same momentum in the mobile app. Joule's integration empowers users on the go to access not only HR information but also broader SAP business content - bringing the power of one unified copilot across SAP systems into the SuccessFactors mobile experience. 

 

                        

                                              

One of the core goals was to unlock a best-in-class user experience. From the beginning, it was clear that users who had already onboarded to the SuccessFactors mobile app shouldn't need to re-authenticate when accessing Joule. Achieving this required alignment across authentication models. While the mobile app previously used IDP activation protocols, Joule required IAS-based tokens. 

These authentication changes were part of a much larger transformation across the SuccessFactors mobile architecture. In part two, we’ll explore how SAP Mobile Services and its SDKs enabled this journey - and what it means for the future. 

Co-authored by @VinodDevarajan @sandeeptds  @Xeric @SamZhang @SamiLechner @_dk @ptawade

entry.title: Say Hi to Joule in SAP SuccessFactors Mobile, Powered by SAP Build — Part 2: Architecture, Execution
entry.content:
In the first part of this blog series, we explored how SAP Joule was introduced into the SAP SuccessFactors Mobile app and what it means for users. In this follow-up, we dive deeper into the architecture, execution approach, and the outlook for the mobile experience. 

SAP BTP is a platform engineered to integrate, automate, extend, and build AI-powered business applications and processes across the enterprise. SAP Build is the unified application development and process automation solution on SAP BTP, enabling personalization and efficiency at scale.

SAP Mobile Services is the channel for mobile development in SAP Build. Developers can use the Mobile Development Kit or SAP BTP SDK for iOS or SAP BTP SDK for Android to create robust, native apps. Administrators can manage the full app lifecycle - from configuring push notifications and securing data to enabling offline access and monitoring usage. SAP apps such as SAP Mobile Start, SAP Ariba, and SAP Service and Asset Manager are all built on this foundational technology stack.

 

The SAP BTP SDK for iOS and SAP BTP SDK for Android provide a reusable component enabling SAP apps to deliver a consistent and native Joule experience across mobile platforms. SAP Mobile Start also integrated this component to bring Joule to its users last year.

SAP SuccessFactors Mobile has long leveraged the Fiori frameworks from the BTP SDKs to provide a consumer-grade experience in an enterprise context. These frameworks follow Fiori design guidelines while maintaining a fully native feel on iOS and Android. Given this deep integration with the BTP SDKs, adopting the Joule component was a natural progression.

As outlined in the first blog, SAP SuccessFactors Mobile supports multiple onboarding mechanisms - QR code, deep link, company search, secret code, and MDM-based provisioning. While this flexibility has been key to serving a diverse user base, it also meant that delivering a seamless, SSO-based Joule experience required a significant architectural shift.

To meet this challenge, SuccessFactors leveraged SAP Mobile Services and the SAP BTP SDKs for iOS and Android. This allowed for secure authentication and efficient routing of Joule-related traffic while maintaining compatibility with existing onboarding flows. The change was foundational - requiring careful planning to avoid disruption for millions of active users worldwide. SAP SuccessFactors Mobile app now connects to both the SuccessFactors (for standard HR data/functions via APIs) and to the Joule service for AI queries.

A key decision in the SAP Mobile Services adoption journey was determining how SAP SuccessFactors Mobile Server would communicate with SAP Mobile Services. A lightweight multi-tenancy model was adopted, allowing SAP Mobile Services to operate behind the scenes while preserving the familiar admin experience within SuccessFactors. New APIs were introduced to maintain this continuity while supporting increased scale.

The adoption of SAP Mobile Services had significant implications for SuccessFactors' client logic and the SAP BTP SDKs for iOS and Android. For instance, the SuccessFactors app transitioned to using the SecureStore Module provided by the BTP SDKs, which securely manages and stores sensitive PII user information on mobile devices. By leveraging this module, SuccessFactors eliminated the need to maintain its own database and reduced reliance on third-party libraries. Another example is that SuccessFactors required administrators to configure preferred browsers via MDM and enforce digits-only passcodes. To accommodate these requirements, the SAP BTP SDKs introduced new capabilities in its Foundation and FioriFlows frameworks.

This collaboration offered mutual benefits, as features developed in response to SuccessFactors' needs were implemented in a reusable way, including enhanced MDM support and new authentication capabilities. As a result, these features became part of the Mobile Services platform offering, benefiting not only other SAP apps but also external customers and partners building their own mobile solutions with SAP Build. The effort also enabled SuccessFactors to audit and simplify its own mobile app, streamlining processes in some cases by retiring underused functionality or tightening security – for instance, limiting the ability to delete other profiles to the primary user.

A massive undertaking of this scope required a phased approach from SuccessFactors, even for features already supported. Future releases will enable SuccessFactors to enable Joule on shared devices.

The foundation laid so far has set the stage for long-term collaboration. For example, SuccessFactors' mobile apps will soon feature an updated home page built using BTP Fiori SDK UI components. We're also planning to enable users to capture photos of documents in response to HR flows in Joule for use cases where attaching certificates or receipts is required. Once synergies are fully realized, SuccessFactors will be well-positioned to leverage more SAP Mobile Services capabilities such as offline functionality. Another exciting area of joint exploration is on-device AI models. As mobile devices become increasingly powerful, both teams are keen to investigate how AI workloads can be intelligently distributed between the cloud and the device - unlocking even more responsive and personalized user experiences.

Please note that all future items mentioned here are indicative and may change subject to SAP's strategic interests.

Beyond the features themselves, this collaborative effort continues to drive SAP’s broader strategic vision of being AI-first and Suite-first. With Joule now live in SAP SuccessFactors Mobile, and with a strong outlook ahead, the foundation is set for a smarter, more unified SAP experience - on every device, for every user.

 

Note: This article has been co-authored by @VinodDevarajan @ptawade @Xeric @SamZhang @SamiLechner @_dk

entry.title: Re: Need help with SAP MAG - XSLT mapping context
entry.content:
The failure is happening on the answer that Priyanka supplied because the target node 'LIN' has 1:1 for the occurrences, so you are telling integration advisor to handle 2:1, which it does by passing the full concatenated node list into the target.  In order to avoid that problem you would want to map the ED1EDL24 segment directly to the appropriate HL loop and your problem will go away (see screenshot for example - can ignore lack of POSNR mapping because our customers want the PO line # which is elsewhere in the IDoc).

 

Regards,

Ryan Crosby
